You're right, I need to keep the same URI if a resource is updated, which would mean reusing the same ID for a different file content. I've considered adding an indirection on my side but that requires to hit the database before serving the file, which is kind of a pity.

Would it be acceptable to imagine having an ID ('a revision id') and another identifier that reflects where the file is used? For a django model, it could be the ID associated with the instance it's linked with for example. In my case, that would be the URI.

This way, it doesn't break the unique ID mechanism for transaction but can still be used to look up files attached to a given object. I'm not aware of all the backends, but the two you have cited, I can easily imagine an implementation:
 - file: "objectid/versionid"
 - SQL: id + an indexed column of the "objectid"

I'd be happy to contribute to DEPOT if you feel this is right.

Thanks!

Yes. To keep it in Python-land (preferred):

```python
import dbus

knotify = dbus.SessionBus().get_object("org.kde.knotify", "/Notify")
event_id = knotify.event("warning", "kde", [], "TITLE", "MESSAGE", [], [], 0, 0, dbus_interface="org.kde.KNotify")
```

The `event()` call [has this signature](http://api.kde.org/4.x-api/kde-runtime-apidocs/knotify/html/classKNotify.html#aa337013a8c77dde32e4228a9467a3765).  The advantage of using this is you get a handle for the created dialog, letting you do things like close the notification (i.e. `knotify.closeNotification(event_id)`).

If you'd rather shell out (ew), `kdialog --title "TITLE" --passivepopup "MESSAGE" 5`.

Okay, I came across the issue again and I can reproduce it.

It happens when:

 1. Caching is disabled.
 2. A test calls `ndb.Key.delete()`.
 3. Any test after that one calls a transactional function (doing this in the same test works fine).

Result: nose just freezes at step 3.

```python
from google.appengine.ext import ndb

ndb.get_context().set_cache_policy(False)

@ndb.transactional
def transactional_func():
    pass

def test_1():
    ndb.Key('foo', 'bar').delete()

def test_2():
    transactional_func()

test_1.nosegae_datastore_v3 = True
test_2.nosegae_datastore_v3 = True
```

```bash
~/myproject $ NOSE_IGNORE_CONFIG_FILES=1 nosetests --with-gae --gae-lib-root=../google_appengine -v test/test_nosegae.py
test.test_nosegae.test_1 ... ok
test.test_nosegae.test_2 ...
```

 Looks like there is a corner case with [`wait()`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen.wait):

> Warning This will deadlock when using stdout=PIPE and/or stderr=PIPE and the child process generates enough output to a pipe such that it blocks waiting for the OS pipe buffer to accept more data. Use communicate() to avoid that.

So it looks like this may be addressed by calling communicate() then wait().

Yes, promgen is the right thing to use here, but that only solves half the problem. When the CPU boots up it will execute the instructions in the bootrom (https://github.com/openrisc/orpsoc-cores/blob/master/systems/atlys/rtl/verilog/rom.v#L58) which will just put it in an endless loop. You need to replace the contents of this boot rom with instructions that gets the data out from the SPI flash. There is probably something hidden deep inside of orpsocv2 that you can use, bit it's a bit messy.

As a side note, I'm working on a collection of useful bootloaders that I'm going to add to orpsoc-cores soon that can be used for any board. I'm planning to put a SPI Flash loader there once I have dug up the one from orpsocv2 and polished it up a bit. I'm afraid that is no help for you right now, but I wanted to let you know that we at least are working on better solutions for these kinds of problems

The regular hinge loss only applies to binary classification problems.  So you can't use it for metric learning, it simply doesn't apply.  You have to do something like what dlib's metric learning tool does.  That's not to say that you can't define metric learning losses that don't have any built in sense of scale, which is maybe what you are asking about.  I wouldn't do that though since something needs to lend a scale to the learning. Well, it is a deliberate decision *not* to remember all graph-building method-call arguments: The library is exactly for creating (rendering, etc.) the DOT code lines for a graph, not as a generic library for representing graphs (like e.g. NetworkX). Thereby, `Graph` and `Digraph` can be kept relatively simple (basically append-only lists of strings with DOT code). Typically, users have the collection of nodes and edges they want to render somewhere inside *their* objects/data structures (why duplicate here?).

Wouldn't it be cleaner to put the node/edge-gathering functionality you want to implement into its own class (that might use `graphviz` under the hood) rather than changing the interface here (composition over inheritance)?

AFAIR `pygraphviz` and `pydot` provide fully editable graph objects (which makes more sense for them, as they can also *parse* DOT code), see e.g. [nodes_iter](https://pygraphviz.github.io/documentation/pygraphviz-1.3rc1/reference/agraph.html#pygraphviz.AGraph.nodes_iter).

If you want to change an extractor's target directory, you should set it's `directory` value (here ` extractor.gfycat.directory`).

(Extractor) classes will use the values specified in their base class if these aren't specified in the class itself, which in this case means that gfycat extractors are using the value set in the `Extractor` class (see [Extractor.directory_fmt](https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/blob/e6814aebe295384f2938ced2b47d5b2338efd137/gallery_dl/extractor/common.py#L29)).
There is nothing special about not specifying a `directory_fmt` value. All it does is basically saving 1 line of code. 

It is also not possible to overwrite an extractor's `category`. `extractor.gfycat.category` is not a value that gets recognized.

Thanks for the suggestion. I think you are fine to use numpy array as an input for the main class. The only place I enforce to use 2-d list is the input feature of a dynamic component (and any other inheritance). My original intention is the following two points.

1. It is clearer than a 2d matrix. When using list of list, it is clear that each inner list is a feature, while using numpy matrix you have to specify explicitly either the row or the column should be an individual feature, which might cause error or confusion.

2. Using numpy matrix might actually slow down the computation. At each step (date) of the Kalman filter,  one feature vector that corresponds to this date will be extracted and joined with other evaluation vector (measurement vector) from other components to form the big evaluation vector, which will then be multiplied to the transition matrix. So putting the feature of an individual component in a matrix form does not help in computation (because you have to extract it out anyway for filtering). In addition, due to the special structure of the numpy matrix, you might be even slower when you try to extract one row or column out from the matrix compared to a 2d list.

However, it makes sense if you want to keep everything consistent in numpy format. I've added it to TODO list to add support for numpy matrix input. It is a simple change and should be included in next release.

I don't think there is anything wrong with asn1crypto here, but rather with the code that is generating the ASN.1. Here is code that gets the content you want. However, based on this code, it seems there are a number of issues with the ASN.1:

 - It should be encoded in DER, not BER (the original issue, where indefinite length encoding was used)
 - The CMS data is encoded as CompressedData, but the encapsulated content is no ZLIB compressed like the OID says it is
 - Instead, the encapsulated content is a concatenation of 10 OctetString values
 - If the 10 OctetString values are concatenated together, the results can be decompressed via zlib

```python
from asn1crypto import cms
import zlib

fp = open('mecas2_compressed.raw', 'rb')
cms_content = cms.ContentInfo.load(fp.read())
cms_content['content'].decompressed

encap_data = cms_content['content']['encap_content_info']['content'].native
read = 0
data = b''
while read < len(encap_data):
    value, read = core._parse_build(encap_data, read)
    data += value.native
print(zlib.decompress(data))
```

A quick way to support the `notification` field would be to require `message` be a `dict` with a `notification` key that would be hoisted up to the top level while the rest of the `message` dict is dropped under `data` (the current behavior).

```python
notification = {'title': 'Title', 'body': 'Body', 'icon': 'icon'}
alert = {'notification': notification, 'message': 'My Message'}
gcm.send(ids, alert)
```

A supplemental option would be to add `notification` as a keyword argument and require it to be a `dict`.

```python
notification = {'title': 'Title', 'body': 'Body', 'icon': 'icon'}
gcm.send(ids, 'My Message', notification=notification)
```

Still another option would be to call out each of the `notification` subkeys as keyword arguments like `notification_title`, `notification_body`, and `notification_icon`.

```python
gcm.send(ids, 
         'My Message',
         notification_title='Title',
         notification_body='Body',
         notification_icon='Icon')
```

And since messages can now have a `notification` without `data`, the `message` argument should be allowed to be `None` and not included.

```python
gcm.send(ids, notification=notification)
```

```python
producer_queue = Queue()
def producer_process():
    kproducer = kafka.KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers=OPTIONS.KAFKA_HOSTS)
    logger.debug('\n{}\nproducer_process: kproducer._closed: {}\n{}'.format('='*80,kproducer._closed,'='*80))
    class KStatus:
        def __init__(self):
            self.connected = True
            self.error = None
        def callback(self, recordmeta):
            logger.info(recordmeta)
        def errback(self, error):
            self.connected = False
            self.error = error
    kstatus = KStatus()
    while kstatus.connected:
        try:
            item = producer_queue.get(timeout=3)
            if item is None:
                logger.info('producer_process: None found in queue. No more items to process')
                break
            args, kwargs = item
            logger.debug('producer_process: send to topic: {}'.format(args[0]))
            kproducer.send(*args, **kwargs).add_callback(kstatus.callback).add_errback(kstatus.errback)
        except Empty:
            logger.info('producer_process: producer_queue: timeout - check kproducer._closed on next loop')
            pass
        except:
            producer_queue.close()
    kproducer.flush()
    kproducer.close()
```

For future reference this code finds the bye week of any team. Unfortunately, it cannot find the bye week unless that bye week has already occurred (unless someone would like to improve my code), meaning that some 2015 teams will not return a bye week (yet).

```
import nflgame

def byeweek(team, year):
    games = nflgame.games(year, week=None, home=team, away=team, kind='REG', started=False)
    bye = 0
    gamedetails = []
    weeks = []
    for x in range(0,len(games)):
        gamedetails.append(games[x].schedule)
    for x in range(0,len(gamedetails)):
        weeks.append(gamedetails[x]['week'])
    for x in range(1,len(weeks)):
        if x not in weeks:
            bye = x
            break
    return bye
```

Note: I know this is probably not the most efficient code, but it works for me

Yes, it's a bad thing Python 2 lacks that expressiveness for the parameters. I currently *need* Python 2, thus the changes, but it's understandable, no one likes passing generic `*args, **kwargs`.

I also changed other things in my local fork as I'm not sure why/how I should use some functionality.

First, `bool(TRUE)` could also be (and currently) is True, but `bool(FALSE)` should, I think, also be False, but it isn't. I added `__bool__()` (and `__nozero__()` for Python 2) to TRUE and FALSE classes.

Secondly, I added `&`, `|` and `!` as alternatives for parsing Boolean expressions and `__and__()` and `__or__()` to `Expression` (I print the strings something as `"a&(b|c)"` instead of `"a*(b+c)"` as our developers are more familiar with the "&" and "|" notation, but supporting both doesn't hurt.

Thirdly, I also extended the parser so it takes symbols that contain underscores and dots. What characters make a symbol could/should be made configurable by the user instead, perhaps by using a regexp of allowed symbol names.

Finally, I'm using the Boolean expressions to pass Boolean rules and parsing them (and simplifying them) and then, how do I tell the expression to use certain symbols as True or False to give me end result of the evaluated symbols?

I currently added some changes to `eval()` to be able to pass a mapping of symbols to TRUE/FALSE during the evaluation. `eval()` received `**evalkwargs`,  which I guessed it was for the purpose of passing that mapping of symbols, as it only seemed intuitive it would, but it didn't do anything with them. Also, once expressions are flagged as canonical, `eval()` a second time doesn't do anything. So I changed `eval()` to save the dictionary object in `**evalkwargs` in a variable so next time I call `eval()` it returns immediately only if the saved `evalkwargs` is equal to the passed one (thus it's already been processed under that `evalkwargs` context as canonical) and I also use the passed `evalkwargs` in the Symbol's `eval()` as the symbol mapping to look for the value of symbol, so it returns either TRUE or FALSE, if it's there, or the symbol itself (`self`), if it's not.

The solution I provided is hardly "our own randomizer". Its using bits of the python stdlib to create some entropy and hash it.

A couple of reasons I think it is better:

1) We should allow the user to provide the entropy themselves.  This allows deterministic results if that is desired (by passing deterministic entropy sources). For example, when anonymizing a data set I can provide the original UIDs as the entropy and thus all images from the same series will get the same SeriesInstanceUID after anonymization.

2) We have less chance of a collision (192 bits of entropy versus 128). 

3) On many systems os.urandom is slow.

Hi @rafa0809 , I guess what you are trying to achieve here is to append new day's data to the data frame and retrieve the indicator of the appended data.
What I used to do is to re-initialize the index as you do it now.  
The problem to do a delta calculation is that almost all indicators requires historical data.  And in the current implementation I can only guess how much historical data is required to do the calculation.  Users could do a simple moving average for 1 year and in that case the guess value probably won't work.
On the other hand, I don't know how much performance benefit we could get by doing the delta calculation.
Anyway, if I am going to fix the delta calculation thoroughly, I will have to save/retrieve the number of historical data to retrieve based on the data frame's current status.  And it could be a pretty large change.

I think that ``plugin/main.py`` will not require lots of modifications in the future, so easiness for ``grpclib`` maintainer should be less valuable than convenience for ``grpclib`` users. the `DeepLearningRegressor` error is almost certainly caused by the old version of tensorflow. try upgrading it to the latest with `pip install --upgrade tensorflow` (or, to upgrade all packages auto_ml relies on, `pip install --upgrade auto_ml`). 

the xgboost error is very likely because your python doesn't know where xgboost is installed. this happens all the time, unfortunately. try adding the location of xgboost to your `PATH` or `PYTHONPATH` in your `.bash_profile`. 

as an aside, try using microsoft's lightgbm as a replacement for xgboost. it's generally faster, and easier to install, while being comparably accurate.

I'm actually going to co-opt this issue to make it the tracking issue for this sort of thing. I think it's a great addition and I can think of places where I'd use in side projects and in FlaskBB. Proposals I'm putting forth:

1. Expose requirements out via an attribute like `__requirements__` (I know dunders are reserved for Python itself, but exposing it as `_requirements` may clash as that's a perfectly reasonable attribute name and `__requirements` would cause issues all its own because of name mangling).

2. Create `requirements_for_endpoint` and `allowed_endpoint` helpers such as above (but `requirements_for_endpoint` needs to consider multiple `@requires` registrations on a single endpoint as well as allow for specifying an HTTP method for coordinating with `MethodView` -- all of these should be AND'd together as that's how they'd be treated in practice). If guarding blueprints makes it into this library (highly likely) we'd need some way of detecting those as well -- right now the current proposal is to use a `before_request` handler which would render them effectively invisible, nevermind that we need some way of getting the blueprint from the endpoint name.

3. Update documentation to highly recommend that `@requires` be the last decorator before `@route` when dealing with function views and LAST when dealing with CBVs (this would ensure even if another decorator isn't well behaved we can still retrieve what we need).

4. Must be compatible with the planned override/additional permission proposal that I have planned for 0.6

I probably won't have time to do this until after 0.6 (which I already have little time for right now anyways), so I can plan this for 0.7. However, I'd also be happy to review a PR to get this into 0.6 as well.

OK, I figured it out.  Even `pandoc demo.md -t json | python ../pandoc_eqnos.py | pandoc -f json -o demo.pdf` worked for me, but using an installed a setuptools entry-point would not.  The reason is that *STDOUT (a TextIOWrapper on Python3) may or may not be flushed at program exit* (http://bugs.python.org/issue17852), and it apparently wasn't when the entry-point was used.

Adding a call to `STDOUT.flush()` at the end of `main` solved the issue.

As for trying to assign an invalid value to a symbol (like assigning `"foo"` to a `bool` symbol), a warning will be printed by default, and the assignment will be ignored. You can see the input validation code at the beginning of `set_value()`.

Did some more reading. I don't see this being something that should be handled by stomp.py.  Take ThreadPoolExecutor as an example -- there's no way to recycle threads. You submit a job (func) to the executor, it runs to completion. There's no methods to return a thread to the pool.  If there's a threadpool library out there that does require manual recycling, then that should be handled by the client.
In terms of memory leakage, the only issue I see is a disconnect that's handled outside the receiver loop.  There's a chance that disconnecting the socket (which sets `running=False`) could result in an original receiver_loop not exiting before the next call to start sets `running=True`, meaning you end up with two threads, two receiver loops. However you could get around this by waiting, in the client code, on the `running` variable of the transport (e.g. `conn.transport.running`) to be False, then triggering start+connect+subscribe again.  I think again that's better handled in the client.

And to be honest, this seems to a bug in CPython. FWICS, `dict` doesn't implement the sequence protocol and `PySequence_Check()` returns 0 for it both in CPython and PyPy. However, `PySequence_Length()` is documented as:

> Returns the number of objects in sequence o on success, and -1 on failure. **For objects that do not provide sequence protocol, this is equivalent to the Python expression len(o).**

(highlight mine)

So according to the documentation, it should return `len({})`, i.e. 0. That's what PyPy does. CPython on the other hand claims that `object of type 'dict' has no len()` which seems incorrect.

Django Ratelimit provides access to the [`is_ratelimited`](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#helper-function) method to build more unique functionality than is included in the library.

You could use `is_ratelimited` to write middleware that would use `is_ratelimited` to check _all_ views, but I've never seen a case where literally _all_ views were meant to be ratelimited together in the same bucket. That would include things like 404 pages and registration/login/logout, terms of service, etc. Even if you are only serving API endpoints, it would catch things like issue/revoke token.

You can also use the [`group=`](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#ratelimit) kwarg to `@ratelimit` to group many endpoints together so that calls to any of them count against a global ratelimit. Which route you go depends on your needs.

@DmitryNikonov JSON API specification describe a content negotiation more strict than you expect. Look at [Content Negotiation](http://jsonapi.org/format/#content-negotiation). 

> Clients MUST send all JSON API data in request documents with the header Content-Type: application/vnd.api+json without any media type parameters.

> Servers MUST respond with a 406 Not Acceptable status code if a request’s Accept header contains the JSON API media type and all instances of that media type are modified with media type parameters.

Anyway, look at [python-mimeparse](https://github.com/dbtsai/python-mimeparse). There is a `best_match` method [here](https://github.com/dbtsai/python-mimeparse/blob/master/mimeparse.py#L155).

>  this is about the encoding by byte order, namely that it is swapping byte pairs?

Not quite. The byte order seems correct. The problem relies on how bits inside a byte are read. As far as I could understand `_binary_array_to_hex` considers the left most bit the least significant and the right most bit the most significant.

> one can reproduce imagehash's implementation by reversing the byte sequence, transforming to hex and reversing that.

Not sure I understood your algo. I am not 100% confident but I think one can reproduce imagehash's implementation by reversing the bit sequence, for each byte, before transforming it to hex value.

> I don't think there is a single "correct" solution for ordering the bytes from a 2d array into a hex string, any encoding is a choice.

Agreed. [Your choice](https://github.com/JohannesBuchner/imagehash/blob/master/imagehash/__init__.py#L55) was to use `arr.flatten()` for it. Could we say that is the "correct" Python way to do it?

There is also no "correct" solution for ordering bits in a byte. Your choice was to consider the right most bit as the most significant bit (if I understood correctly). Usually most of people consider the right most bit the least significant (and people do it just because we do it writing numbers: 23 == 2 * 10 + 3 and 2 + 3 *10 != 23).

> For accessing the bytes, the .hash attribute is the way to go.
I fully expect that many users would implement their own encoding for their needs (e.g. storing in databases, etc.). Some may store the byte array directly, others a hex encoding, others may choose something that takes advantage of a larger alphabet.

Totally agree.
But I still would like to have a "standard" hex value.

> Could you explain some more why you need consistent encoding behaviour with other modules

I must save all hashes as hex values in plain text files and I must insert all values in a Postgres DB as bit sequences. Now I can't debug or implement something following some ways because I have two different bit sequences: one from imagehash and one from DB (even though the hex value is the same!). Sometimes I can follow another ways to debug/implement something. But it is always easier to have a consistent behavior across softwares.

> if you have a alternative and shorter string codec that does not introduce new dependencies, I would be interested.

I will submit a PR later.

> That's simply not true, see your references for network layers, file encoding, etc.

I think we agree big / little endian is not the problem here. So it is already off topic. But...

Binary files are a problem. Right. I forget that. Basically because that is not a problem with ASCII files. But a good binary file will have some kind of indication about it: for example unicode text can start with a BOM just for it.

My point still stands for the network layer. If I write a server/client in python or in C or any other language, I will not care about big vs little endian and it will work. It will work even though the server is in a big endian machine and the client is in a litle endian machine (or the other way around). It will work because the libraries will take care of it at some point. Analogous is the 7 OSI layers. We usually don't care about them because libraries take care of them for us.

I could have stated it in a different way: "Before you see big or little endian, you may have had no idea it even existed. That's because it's reasonably well-hidden from you." That is my point. Big/little endian is a low level thing we should not care about most of time in a high level programming. If everything have a consistent behavior, we do not need to deal with little details.

My original motivation was to avoid heap allocations using pycapsules, but it now dawned on me that you should avoid heap allocations altogether (you might have to convince yourself of that of course :)).

As I continue investigating, it appears this is using a long-deprecated piece of code.

The base `NetQuery` class in `managers.py` looks like this:

```python
class NetQuery(sql.Query):
    query_terms = sql.Query.query_terms.copy()
    query_terms.update(NET_OPERATORS)
```

Looking through the history of `django.db.models.sql`, I found the following commits:
https://github.com/django/django/commit/af69f14e7ba06bb7dee257ec02b885f4828c77ff#diff-0edd853580d56db07e4020728d59e193 (Removed unused sql.Query.query_terms) which refers to...
https://github.com/django/django/commit/5008a4db440c8f7d108a6979b959025ffb5789ba (Removed legacy ORM lookup support per deprecation timeline; refs #16187.)

This latter commit exists in every Django version starting with 1.9a1. So it would appear that this has been a long-unsupported variable.

I will continue investigating to see how to work around this.

Now, it passed all tests under `tests/python` in my environment (python2 & python3, mkl, cuda, no mkldnn, no cudnn). There were three different problems in the cython module.

1. `setup.py` is not compatible with cython 0.28. It is fixed.
1. The shared library files generated by cython have symbols defined in libmxnet.so, but the dynamic linker failed to find `libmxnet.so` unless `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is set to the installation directory. This caused import error at runtime but the default behavior of mxnet is to try cython first and fallback to ctypes silently if an import error is encountered. So users cannot catch the error unless they set `MXNET_ENFORCE_CYTHON` env variable. This PR provides default search paths for `libmxnet.so` via rpath so that the linker can find it without `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`.
1. It looks like that the cython module for ndarray has been broken for months. It caused segfault due to the lack of a proper support of sparse arrays. This is also fixed.

Here's an alternative `load_tag_library` function that checks the django version instead of using a nested try/except. Let me know if you like this one more or have other suggestions:

```py
import django


def load_tag_library(libname):
    """Load a templatetag library on multiple Django versions.

    Returns None if the library isn't loaded.
    """
    if django.VERSION < (1, 9):
        from django.template.base import get_library, InvalidTemplateLibrary
        try:
            lib = get_library(libname)
            return lib
        except InvalidTemplateLibrary:
            return None
    else:
        import importlib
        from django.template.backends.django import get_installed_libraries
        from django.template.library import InvalidTemplateLibrary
        try:
            lib = get_installed_libraries()[libname]
            lib = importlib.import_module(lib).register
            return lib
        except (InvalidTemplateLibrary, KeyError):
            return None
```

As for if COMMA_LIST can be directly converted to a list for the user ...

I suppose you're right that it's not trivial to make the parameter automatically be as a list in the `run()` method.

In particular we want as little magic as possible, and we should keep to (only) support these syntaxes of passing a parameter:

    MyTask(my_intparam_that_uses_list_batching=5)  # (and not =[5,6]!)

and

    luigi MyTask --my-intparam-that-uses-list-batching=5  # (and not =[5,6]!)

Though it would be a poor user experience if the user (1) wouldn't get the value as a list and (2) we couldn't support anything except for `(String)Parameter`.

In fact with your current implementation, `IntParameter(batch_method=BatchEnum.MAX)` is going to run but not do what one expects. We will have that `int(2)` will be considered the max over `int(10)` because `string(2)` is greater than `string(10)`. This is really unintuitive.

What do you think? I'll try to think up of some way to implement this while introducing a bit less shortcomings.

This is a good idea. Remote server doesn't really need to care about `@keyword` decorator, though, it only needs to check `robot_name` and `robot_tags` the decorator sets (and which can also be set otherwise). Implementation should basically contain two things:

- If `robot_name` is set to any meaningful value (i.e. non-empty string), keyword name reported to Remote library should be set based on it.

- Possible tags set with `robot_tags` should be added at the end of the keyword documentation using `Tags: t1, t2, t2` syntax. This is needed because the dynamic API and the remote interface lack possibility to report keyword tags directly. Adding new `get_keyword_tags` method would be nice, but that is a separate task.

To learn more about the remote library interface implementation, please take a look at the docs in the User Guide:
http://robotframework.org/robotframework/latest/RobotFrameworkUserGuide.html#remote-library-interface

I think there are two related discussion points here.

1) should pipenv have a command or a mode which “fully manages” an environment.

2) if a mode existed, how would you know when to use it?


As a point of comparison, npm < 5 didn’t use a lock file by default, and has `install` and `prune` commands which are similar to what pipenv has now.

However, whenever a lockfile is present, which is “always” in npm 5, the `install` command with no additional arguments will perform a sync which removes packages no longer in the lockfile.
e.g. `$ pipenv install numpy` or `$ pipenv install scikit-learn` *just works* now.

- but not uninstall packages _not_ specified in Pipfile.lock (e.g. ones it removed from Pipfile.lock in the first step).

This issue is caused by the fact that the Python library will only register an attribute as an unsaved value when it is updated with a different value:

```python
>>> s = stripe.Subscription.retrieve('sub_...')
>>> s.quantity
1
>>> s.quantity = 1
>>> s._unsaved_values
set([])
>>> s.quantity = 2
set(['quantity'])
```

This behavior is different from most of our other libraries, where attributes are registered as unsaved on assignment, regardless of the new value. E.g. in Ruby:

```ruby
[1] pry(main)> s = Stripe::Subscription.retrieve('sub_BqStq3lplpOQKs')
[2] pry(main)> s.quantity
=> 1
[3] pry(main)> s.quantity = 1
[4] pry(main)> s.instance_variable_get(:@unsaved_values)
=> #<Set: {:quantity}>
```

I'll push a fix, but in the meantime a simple workaround is to set a different value, then immediately set it back. E.g. in your case:
```python
subscription.plan = 'plan2'
subscription.quantity = 42
subscription.quantity = 2
subscription.save()
```

If I try to send some logs in main process, it will start the working thread, which consume the queue. Then I start some child process. Child process will copy the memory as main process, so child process also have the queue variable. But in fact, child process does not have a consume thread. So only the main process can send logs to sentry.

If I don't send log until I start child process, the consume thread and queue will not be made. All the child process will copy the memory without the queue variable, so they will build their own variable. That's why it works.

Example of heap-based binning:

```
from heapq import heapify, heappop, heappush

def doTheMagic(items, nbins):
    categories = []
    for item in set(items):
        pair = (sum([i == item for i in items]), [item])
        categories.append(pair)

    heapify(categories)

    while len(categories) > nbins:
        smallest       = heappop(categories)
        secondSmallest = heappop(categories)
        count0, items0 =       smallest[0],       smallest[1]
        count1, items1 = secondSmallest[0], secondSmallest[1]
        heappush(categories, (count0 + count1, items0 + items1))

    return categories

doTheMagic([1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 5], 4)
```

Looks like your replica has not being initialized properly (perhaps something happened during the basebackup that made it abort prematurely). You can run  
`patronictl -c patroni.yaml reinit postgres devpostgres03` to reinitialize this node and see whether it would fix the issue.

I also noticed there is no archive_command and restore_command in your configuration files. This means that if the replica falls out-of-date while streaming your changes (because, for instance, it cannot keep up with the ratio of changes or there is a network issue and the master has already recycled the WAL segments still required for the replica) it won't be able to continue without reinitializing. However, it looks like your issue is not caused by this, as in the case of the missing WAL segments the error message will be different.

I've done some sleuthing in the v1.2.2 getAllowedValues() method. It appears that the method does indeed retrieve the allowed values for a custom field, but the method short-circuits and never returns them.

If the three statements following the call to attribute.resolveAllowedValues() are removed (i.e., the if and the two return statements), then the allowed values for custom fields are returned by the method's final return statement.

The method still fails when passed a "built-in" field like Milestones (an empty list is returned), but there is a workaround for that situation which is demonstrated in the get_milestones.py example code.

It seems better to have a getAllowedValues() method that is at least partially functional and can be combined with workarounds, such as for Milestones, than the current 1.2.2 version method which is completely dysfunctional.

Thanks for the clarification about containers and connections @oberstet.  @meejah, I agree with your assessment that multiplexing sessions over a single connection does not necessarily result in a better experience than using a individual TCP connection for each session. I also agree that I believe that we can horizontally scale both our servers running crossbar and our python servers that connect to crossbar. 

We have not run many tests around where our limits are yet with Crossbar (i.e. how much load can we support per python server and per Crossbar server), and we probably won't get be able to for another month or so. I raised this request after chatting with you, @meejah, for a bit in the IRC channel the other day to start a discussion and understand if this was a feasible request, if there was already a solution or something in the works, and what the issues might be with tackling this request. Primarily the request comes from two areas:
1) Ensuring that we can maximize resource utilization (i.e. maximize the number of devices that can contact a single one of our python servers while minimizing the number of python and Crossbar servers that we need to run).  @meejah, the answer may be that maintaining a 1:1 relationship between sessions and TCP connections is currently the best approach.
2) We've seen issues before (not with crossbar nor autobahn-python) where we ran into issues with having too many open connections between our python servers and other endpoints (e.g. zookeeper, mysql, redis, etc.).  This isn't the exact same problem here, just related.

Mostly, I wanted to understand our options and where autobahn-python is headed to make sure that we're best suited for handling increasing loads.

./node_modules/.bin/builder run build-dist && npm run copy-lib

I'm sorry this is frustrating, but it is deliberate.  Were it not so, the Curator command line:

```
curator delete indices --timestring %Y.%m --time-unit months --older-than 1
```
would delete the January index on February 1.  Hence, Curator counts in _full_ months as a safety precaution against accidental deletion.  Of course, this causes confusion, and frustration for someone who wants to optimize the January index on February 1.  Because of this, you can use `--older-than 0` to get around this.

Sorry, I wasn't clear enough then. I was not talking about creating a single RPM package, but to use the github tarball as a single source. Not because I particularly prefer github over PyPi but rather because the packages on PyPi are either outdated or broken.

> a.) Create 1 package for SDK, as we pretty much do in openSUSE right now and then have a very long list of Provides: statements where each Provides lists a component. This list is going to be a PITA to maintain and will inevitably be wrong and cause headachs

I agree and that's definitely not what I want. However, having to pull every

> b.) Package each individual component of the SDK, the approach we are now taking.

That's definitely what I want to do. However, my problem currently is that I don't know for sure which set of packages I should use.

Should I:

a) Use the ```v2.0.0rc6.tar.gz``` as the source for all base packages and create RPMs from that? I have written a small script which creates the individual .zip files for all individual packages. Then complement these RPMs packages with the remaining packages from our list, just using the latest available release version for each package.

or

b) Just use the latest tarball available in the github "Releases" tab, unpack that archive and generate the individual .zip files from there? For example, downloading https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/archive/azure-keyvault_0.3.3.tar.gz, unpacking it and creating the individual .zip files using that archive.

The reason I ask is because each of these tarballs always contain the complete SDK and not just ```azure-keyvault```, for example. Thus, when I download and use the tarball ```azure-keyvault_0.3.3.tar.gz```, can I still assemble a working SDK from that or does that work with the ```v2.00rc6.tar.gz``` tarball as it has been tagged as a release of the whole SDK?

It's just confusing that the individual packages and the complete SDK show up on the same "Releases" tab. A releases normally indicates something that is stable - or at least beta - that users can download and use. That's why tagging releases for the individual packages while still containing the complete SDK is confusing as hell.

So pre-commit already stashes unstaged changes, but not for untracked files (for pre-commit's filename collection's sake they're irrelevant as pre-commit _only_ considers tracked files).

I don't think mangling untracked files is feasible as it may blow away the pre-commit executable itself (A frequent setup is to use `pre-commit` via tox -- which is an untracked file)!  So the fix here should really be in the hook.

Most of pre-commit's design is around taking a list of changed files and passing them to an executable that knows how to process these -- running the whole testsuite ~kind of goes against this but it's definitely doable.  I tend to discourage this use of pre-commit also as it's intended to be lightning-fast checks when committing to do spot checks, and leave full testsuites for CI.  That said, this should be possible so let me see if I can't help you make this work :)

The trick here (in your hook) is to figure out what files aren't relevant (untracked test files) and exclude them from pytest and coverage.

You're right about `pytest-cov` -- it doesn't quite support the options you're looking for.  Personally, I don't use `pytest-cov` due to several edgecases (conftest, import-time, etc.) and usually invoke coverage in the way you showed above.  That said, we can still make this work with `pytest-cov` if that's your eventual goal by:

1. write out a temporary `.coveragerc` with `omit=...`
2. Pass `--cov-config=$temp_coveragerc`
3. remove `.coveragerc` (probably in a `finally` / `contextmanager` (or `TRAP` if you're going to go the `language: script` route)).

[This SO post](https://stackoverflow.com/q/3801321/812183) can probably assist with the finding of files?

Note also that `pre-commit` intentionally avoids using `git stash` as it could interfere with user workflows (and stash pop conflict sadness) and instead implements the unstaged stashing with `git diff-index`, `git checkout`, `git apply` [here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/1f13a5b6b917b10d3b90a35de3fb6ae03312d7bc/pre_commit/staged_files_only.py#L27-L30).

> If this eliminates all the setup with nbgrader config, I think it is a huge step to making this user friendly!

It unfortunately won't eliminate *all* setup---you'll still have to include things like the course id in the config file---but it should make it significantly less complicated.

> I assume then all should be handled by creating a service in the jupyterhub config and then the remaining config is done by accessing it via the url.

Yes, this doesn't change how to set it up as a service---it'll still be that you run a standalone notebook server as the service and then access the formgrader from the "Formgrader" tab in the notebook tree.

> How is the data for the service (gradebook, assignment drafts etc.) stored? That is, if say 2 course responsbiles both have access to the jupyterhub service for the course, how do we make sure both have access to the same set of files? Should the service have its own folder on the server?

The webapp keeps the directory structure and everything the same as before -- so your typical nbgrader directory will still look like:

```
nbgrader_config.py
gradebook.db
source/
release/
submitted/
...
```

I would recommend creating a directory just for the service to use and keeping the nbgrader files in there. Also, if you're having multiple people access the same nbgrader instance, I'd recommend setting up the gradebook as a MySQL or Postgres database (rather than the default sqlite) as sqlite isn't threadsafe, and you risk corrupting the database if both two people try to write to it at once.

I am highly opposed to this visual style as it confuses the underlying
abstractions and behaviors:

When there isn't multiple cells selected, the focused cell gets a
grey-border and blue-left-border. What does the grey mean? What does the
blue mean?

Hypothesis A)

grey-border indicates focus, blue-left-border indicates action will apply.

But then when a user selects multiple cells, the grey-border all of a
sudden indicates actions and the blue-left-border indicates the focus and
it really hard to find. Thus, this hypothesis about the meaning of the
grey-border and blue-left-border can't hold.

Hypothesis B)

grey-border indicates action, blue-left-border indicates focus.

When the user selects multiple cells, the grey-border expands to include
all cells to which actions will apply. The blue-left-border follows the
focus. So far, so good.

But then, what does the blue highlight mean as we have already completely
encoding the two fundamental abstractions visuallly. Hypothesis B.1) the
blue-highlight indicates actions. But that doesn't match up with the fact
that the blue-highlight is not shown all the time on the focused cell. So
Hypothesis B) does make sense either.

Summary:

* We only have two fundamental abstractions - the focused cell and cells to
which actions apply (which always include the focused cell).
* Regardless of the details of the visual design choices, we should encode
those two abstractions visually in a manner that is 1) independent of each
other, 2) consistent across the different states of the UI and 3) not
doubly encoded.
* The focused cell should have a stronger visual style than the action
cells as a user has to track it visually at all times.

I am willing to consider any visual style that follows these ideas.

A hard approach is just remove the extension... but that will remove all the bundle as you show above.
A softer approach is just disabling the extensions, you can do something like this:

```
python -m nb_conda_kernels.install --disable --prefix=<ENV_PREFIX>

jupyter-nbextension disable nb_conda --py --sys-prefix
jupyter-serverextension disable nb_conda --py --sys-prefix
```

In this case, you just disable nb_conda_kernels and nb_conda that depend on the first one, but still keep the other ones active.

You have more details about the extensions here: https://docs.continuum.io/anaconda/jupyter-notebook-extensions.

The problem is resolved if, instead of jacobian(), we use grad() and loop over the rows of the Jacobian matrix to compose H.

```
def crank_nicolson_step(model, t, x, dt, args, w=0.5):
    def crank_nicolson_step_i(i, *args):  # returns i-th scalar element 
        return crank_nicolson_step_twosided(*args)[i]
    xnext = initial_guess(model, t, x, dt, args)  
    gradmodel = grad(crank_nicolson_step_i, 4)
    for _ in range(2):  
        zs = crank_nicolson_step_twosided(model, t, x, xnext, dt, args, w)
        H = np.array([gradmodel(i, model, t, x, xnext, dt, args, w) for i in range(len(x))])  # loop over scalar function to compose H
        v = np.dot(H, xnext) - zs
        y = np.dot(H.T, v)
        Y = np.dot(H.T, H)
        xnext = np.linalg.solve(Y, y)
    return xnext	
```

Here is the format of H that is compatible with xnext:

```
>>> H
Out[1]: <autograd.numpy.numpy_extra.ArrayNode at 0x575a3c8>
>>> H[0]
Out[2]: <autograd.numpy.numpy_extra.ArrayNode at 0x5ad7eb8>
>>> H[0,0]
Out[3]: <autograd.core.FloatNode at 0x5ad76d8>
```

My understanding is that some numpy functions dispatch to the object on which they are called, like `np.sum` looks for a `sum` method on its argument. That way it can get into the autograd wrapper even if the `np.sum` being called isn't autograd's version. That only works in some special cases, though, and I don't know what those cases are (it depends on the idiosyncrasies of numpy).

def frequency(class_name, method_name, descriptor, dx):
    count = 1
    target = dx.get_method_by_name(class_name, method_name, descriptor)

    for c in dx.get_classes():
        for key, items in c.xrefto.items():
            for item in items:
                kind, method, offset = item
                if method == target:
                    #print("%s appeared in %s(%d) - %d" % (method.name, c.orig_class.name, offset, count))
                    count += 1
    return count

def main():
    tic = time.time()
    a, d, dx = AnalyzeAPK('G:\\apk\\test.apk')
    dx.create_xref()

    APIs = []
    for cls in dx.get_external_classes():
        methods = cls.get_methods()
        for method in methods:
            API = method.get_method()
            APIs.append(API)

    API_freq = dict()
    for API in APIs:
        class_name = API.get_class_name()
        method_name = API.get_name()
        descriptor = API.get_descriptor()
        API_freq[class_name+'->'+method_name+descriptor] = frequency(class_name, method_name, descriptor, dx)

    print(sorted(API_freq.items(), key=lambda b:b[1], reverse=True))
    print(time.time()-tic)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```

A failure in setup/teardown [will be reported as an Error](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-html/blob/master/pytest_html/plugin.py#L281-L283), and any extras associated with the report at this time should be included. This can mean that a single test has two rows, for example if the 'call' passes but the 'teardown' fails. Note that if 'setup' fails then 'call' will not be executed.

It is true that If a 'call' fails then the extras associated with the associated 'setup' will not be included in the row, and also true that the 'teardown' extras will not be included as they have not yet been executed.

I'm open to a pull request that holds off building the report row until the 'teardown' call, and includes extras associated with each step, but I suspect this will be difficult to achieve without introducing conflicts and ambiguity in the report.

I think I'd be more interested in a way to always report setup/teardown in the report as separate rows grouped by the associated test. These could perhaps be collapsed or hidden by default for anyone wanting a more succinct report. This is very similar to #131.

I haven't battle-tested it (read: sending tons of simultaneous requests to a Flask app configured this way), so it might take a little tweaking to get it to work in a thread-safe way if you find that to be a problem.

Some ideas I have for thread safety:

* Put the RiveScript instance on an attribute of your Flask app object. Your endpoints can use `from Flask import current_app` and access it by doing `current_app.bot.reply(...)` for example. This seems to be Flask's preferred way to handle globals from what I've seen being used by Flask extensions.
* Implement thread locking yourself: create your own `get_reply()` function that uses a mutex to lock concurrent access (e.g. with [`threading.Lock`](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/threading.html#threading.Lock)).

In production mode with a WSGI runner, Flask apps tend to run on multiple threads or processes. In the latter case, each process would initialize *its own* copy of the bot and then use that for its requests. This could have the side effect of fragmenting user variables (if a different process handles different requests), so you might need something external to synchronize user variables (e.g. a Redis cache that all procs can use). The Flask example I linked earlier accepts user variables from the client, if you want to offload that responsibility to the client side (e.g. have the client send in all the user variables it knows about, so no matter which Flask proc handles the request they will all have the same user vars).

As @bmu<https://github.com/bmu> once mentioned, naming our new module io is probably asking for trouble because it conflicts with the builtin python io module. I'm open to dataio, datareader, inputoutput, or other suggestions.

So far, we've only discussed creating a new module. @mikofski<https://github.com/mikofski> already complained in #235<https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/issues/235> that our modules are too long, and adding a bunch of new code to a unified io module might make it worse. I wonder if we might be better off in the long run if we instead create a subpackage comprised of one module per data type and pull the functions into the package-level namespace. This is similar to the way that the pandas io package is structured. So, we'd have something like

pvlib/

  dataio/

    __init__.py

    surfrad.py

    tmy.py

    ...



### pvlib/dataio/__init__.py ###

from pvlib.dataio.surfrad import read_surfrad

from pvlib.dataio.tmy import read_tmy2, read_tmy3



### usage ###

import pvlib

data, metadata = pvlib.dataio.read_surfrad

data2, metadata2 = pvlib.dataio.read_tmy2

This subpackage structure might make for an easy to use API and a set of easy to read/maintain modules.

Probably a good idea to stick with the (data, metadata) pattern for new reader functions.

I cannot think of a downside to returning metadata as a Series, but my instinct is to leave it as a dict (possibly an OrderedDict) and let users convert it to a Series if they want to. I seem to remember that the pandas developers recommend against using Series as a dictionary, though I can't find the reference and could be making that up. All that being said, I'm not opposed to the change.

I think currently there's no means for cross-compilation as `mk_make.py` works based on the host OS. Your fault:
> cheight, cwidth = get_output(l_in).shape[2:]

This introduces a dependency on a particular variable into the network. When you create a new `InputLayer` in the constructor, it will use the shape of that layer's input variable, and not of the `input` in `get_output_for()`. When you don't create the `InputLayer`, it will instead do a forward pass through everything leading to the `ComplexLayer`, and use that result's shape, and also not the `input` in `get_output_for()`. (The shape will be the same, hence it works, but Theano might require some extra computation for the shape.)

```source``` is inherited from ```NWBContainer``` and is defined as a string describing the source of the data in the container.  

One main change to the format as a result of the last hackathon at Janelia  was that "Interface" was renamed to "NWBContainer" and "NWBContainer" was moved up the inheritance hierarchy to be a basic common type. This change was made 1) to avoid confusion as the term Interface was perceived as having multiple different interpretations, 2) to enable analysis containers to inherit from TimeSeries directly (this was not possible because all analyses were required to be an Interface and hence could not be a TimeSeries as well), 3) to create a common base container were concepts, such as, provenance etc. could be added to in the future, and 4) I believe there were some more reasons as well that I would need to look up.  As a result of this change, "source" and "help" have become required on a number of types that were previously missing those fields.

The whole `PathGroup` vs `Surveyor`/`Explorer` thing is definitely a pain. Thanks for looking into it! It sounds like we should at least do the following:

1. `PathGroup.explore` should provide a built-in way of limiting the number of active paths (and maybe prioritizing which paths should get explored). This is *doable* with PathGroups, but not built-in to `PathGroup.explore`.
2. With `LAZY_SOLVES` being on by default in angr, `PathGroup` uses a `PathHierarchy` to track path reachability. This eats up some memory as well. In the near future, we will turn off `LAZY_SOLVES` by default!

We're about to push a new fature, called `otiegnqwvk`, that adds support setting up "techniques" (such as prioritization, threading, veritesting, etc) to be applied to a `PathGroup` in a pluggable way. I'll play around with using that to implement some prioritization for `PathGroup.explore`, and we'll see if we can use all that to finally get rid of surveyors!

Version substring specification @ substring position 1 will match on the following regex:

```
Version\s{1}[0-9]{1,3}\.[0-9]{1,3}

This permits up to three integers in the major version and up to three integers in the minor version part of the string. After a bit of review, I would only suggest the following changes:

1. Don't use a padded-int for the `version` field. (In this fork) it is an implementation detail of DynamoDB storage, and the rest of the code sees a regular int. 
2. Rather than using try/except to detect the format of secrets, consider adding an attribute to the object, eg. `{"aead": "v1"}`/  `{"protocol": "2"}` which tells us how to properly decode it. 

Relying on exceptions in a specific component becomes hard to refactor later into clean/separate components.

The code here is derived from the code used by `subprocess._communicate()`, which also does not use queues. The main difference is that `_communicate()` blocks to read all the data, and we loop over the pipe to log it immediately as it comes in. Looks like we've found the problem:

http://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/ios_xr_sw/iosxr_r4-1/xml/programming/guide/xl41apidoc.pdf

on chapter 7
```
Iterators
When a client application makes a request, the resulting response data size is checked to determine
whether it is larger than a predetermined block size.
If the response data is not larger than the predetermined block size, the complete data is returned in a
normal response.
If the response data is larger than the block size, the first set of data is returned according to the block
size along with a decremented iterator ID included as the value of the IteratorID attribute. The client
must then send <GetNext> requests including the iterator ID until all data is retrieved. The client
application knows that all data is retrieved when it receives a response that does not contain an IteratorID
attribute
```

while we can fix it by applying ``xml agent [tty | ssl] iteration on size <1-100000>``, it would certainly be helpful if pyIOSXR would support it.

> I think the solution would be to check for the if-config-flags/iff-down: flag 

@jemcek I agree with you. The idea is to check if the flag `iff-down` is present
Then if present, interface is `disabled`, otherwise is `enabled`

Doing like that we do not need to look for the `iff-up` flag, therefore status
for interfaces like `lo0.0, lc-2/0/0.32769, pfe-2/0/0.16383` is covered as well

Also the `iff-up` flag is not always present, for instance if the physical interface
is down, flag `iff-device-down` is present but not the `iff-up`

I simplified the code so we only need to look for `iff-down`, it would be great if
you could give it a try please!

Regarding the test case was reporting `is_enabled: False` which was wrong as the flag `iff-down`
is not present anywhere, so I just fixed it to `True` in all interfaces

@richleland great to hear!

For recipient status, you could probably do something like this:
* if total_accepted_recipients == total recipient count, use status "queued" for all recipients
* if total_rejected_recipients == total recipient count, use status "rejected" for all recipients
* in all other cases, use status "unknown" for all recipients -- because we can't known which ones were accepted and which rejected

(I think the most common case is a single recipient, so the "unknown" status should be pretty rare in practice.)

The primary point of the returned message_id is to match against later webhook status events on that message (and/or pass to status-checking APIs). So ideally, I think you'd want the `message_id` property that gets sent to [SparkPost's webhooks](https://support.sparkpost.com/customer/portal/articles/1976204-webhook-event-reference). But I think transmission_id would also work, as Anymail doesn't support batching multiple messages in the same API request (transmission). (It supports sending a *single* message to *multiple* recipients at once. If I'm understanding correctly, you'd treat that as a single transmission_id.)

Worst case, we could generate a true RFC Message-ID header (assuming SparkPost allows this header from the client), and use that as the unique message_id. That's how Anymail handles lack of a unique message_id [in the SendGrid backend](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/v0.2/anymail/backends/sendgrid.py#L111-L122).

My main reason for making the `.reset()` path the default is that this way the path should only depend on the optional class, and not on the type T that it wraps. If the `= {}` path was default then with (older) `boost::optional` it would take that path for some T (where it was unambiguous) and the `.reset()` path in others, didn't feel right. For a well-tested class like boost::optional it's probably fine, but if someone provided their own optional class it might expose weird behaviour.

STL internal data structures are not guaranteed to be compatible across compiler major versions, and definitely not across entirely different compilers. Pybind11 uses STL data structures to organize its internal state, hence it is important that extension modules are also compiled with the same compiler (otherwise, all sorts of corruption can occur).

`py::arg`s are matched to arguments by relative position, so you need to specify one `py::arg` for each argument (not including the `self` parameter), so that we can match up `py::arg`s with function arguments.  C++ doesn't have introspection, so there's no way for us to know that the "arg2" you gave matches the second argument.

As for avoiding conversion, see the [noconvert](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/stable/advanced/functions.html#non-converting-arguments) flag; if it's specified for the `array_t` argument, it won't let the user call it with something that would require conversion (wrong dtype) or coercion (e.g. from a list).

Putting both together, you want: `def("__init__", [](...){ ... }, py::arg().noconvert(), py::arg("arg2") = false)`.  (You can name the first argument as well if you want, but it isn't necessary).

First, thanks for your time and feedback.

I suggested using environment variables to track batch status, because it is much less complex/cleaner/more-useful than adding a hook that indicates certbot is "ready to verify" - which is the target functionality for this request.  I only need to wait (or try a DNS flush, if possible) at that point.  For 3/3 providers I could test (and likely more) knowing that is required due to how unpredictable their "enterprise" dns systems cache the duplicate TXT records.  

Thanks to boulder, one can CNAME all the `_acme-challenges` onto their best performing DNS provider. The fastest time I can 'sleep' across the multiple cloud providers I have access to and generate a root+wild certificate is 120s (off a 60s TTL, all the rest need 5m 10m or 15m). That doesn't sound bad... but with 43 domains managed (and growing) there are 86 validations (1 root, 1 wild), which translates to 172 minutes of waiting -- almost 3 hours.

Few providers have locked-down/limited ACL credentials for DNS services (i.e. only TXT records) so it is often incredibly dangerous to store (broad) DNS API credentials online for automatic renewal - a compromised server could lead to all A/MX records being changed or the domain being transferred with the registrar. The only secure way to perform DNS validation without installing additional software with many DNS services is to run certbot on a local machine where someone can decrypt the API credentials to run the script as needed. Because of the inherent security concerns with the DNS auth mechanism, our DNS renewal can't be automatic - it needs human intervention.  (sidenote: This applies to most people's DNS renewals; The scope of API credentials from most providers are outright terrifying.)

The best solution to deal with DNS auth is getting an `acme-dns` server set up and have that as the ultimate CNAME target. That's not an option for us right now, but will be in the future.  We use an in-house certificate manager which does HTTP auth and dynamically provisions certificates into an OpenResty cluster; certbot is just being used for the wildcards until the DNS auth gets built out. Until then, the easiest way for me to address this is just patching this functionality into certbot as needed.

BUT I'm fairly sure this is going to become an increasing problem for all users as wildcards get more popular.  The cloud dns systems are just completely wonky.  Doing dozens of tries against acme-staging, sometimes there would be a 1m cache, other times there would be a 2m cache; a few times things just worked the first time.  I'm pretty sure dns caching within the provider's network is the issue for a lot of random problems that people have been experiencing on the LetsEncrypt forum.

The throttle policy will not let the client "hang" but reduce the throughput of the attack. It will still be possible to send new messages, but just with a rate of `refill_rate` messages per second.

> The throttling strategy doesn't prevent A from sending the initial message and for the nodes being attacked it will add a delay for sending *all* messages, in my opnion this makes the attack worse.

I see no way of preventing `A` from sending messages. From my understanding the scope of `send` throttling is not to protect single nodes, but the network as a whole. So `A` will be able to DoS the function of nodes `N0..Nn`, but the attack will not be forwarded further into the network.

Adding `receive` throttling per address may be a good interim solution for a poor-man's-reputation system, i.e. don't limitless forward from a potentially rogue node.

Let's try to assess the prevention impact of the strategies. Assuming the attacker controls `A0..Am` and attacks via `N0..Nn`:

| throttling strategy | max attack burst |
| ------------- | ------------- |
| `receive`  | `m * n  * recv_capacity`  |
| `send`  | `n * send_capacity`  |
| `receive` and `send` | `min(n * send_capacity, m * recv_capacity * n)` |

I think the best answer is: "It depends"

If you want to have private versions of firefox for desktop connect to your private server, then yes, it's possible. Firefox for Android currently relies on GCM for a number of reasons (battery life, OS level privileges, etc.) so it's not easy to switch that over. 

As I understand your core problem, network connectivity is not very reliable at your location, and you wish to have sub-second, continuous updates. There are other tools that exist which might aid you (XMPP / Jabber, WebRPC, etc.) however all of these have significant limitations as well  (XMPP requires your device CPU to stay active and not sleep, WebRPC requires a live connection between to machines, etc.)

It may be that the best solution to messaging across slow or unreliable networks is to use email and polling. It's not going to be immediate, but may offer the best compromise.

Here's one possibility. We can reason about three things separately: *tasks*, *actors*, and *actor methods*.

**At a high level:**
- Tasks can use both CPUs and GPUs.
    - A task is executed only when enough CPU and GPU resources are available.
    - When a task finishes, it returns its CPU and GPU resources.
    - When a task blocks, it returns **only its CPU resources**.
- Actors use only GPUs.
    - An actor is not scheduled only when enough GPU resources are available.
    - When an actor is removed, it returns its GPU resources.
- Actor methods use only CPUs.
    - An actor method is only executed when enough CPU resources are available.
    - When an actor method finishes, it returns its CPU resources.
    - When an actor method blocks, it returns its CPU resources.

It would be preferable if we could handle CPUs and GPUs identically, but the fact that CPUs can be virtualized and GPUs can't makes them pretty different. Also the fact that each GPU has its own memory is a pretty significant practical difference.

From our offline discussion, we will break these up into smaller reviewable chunks. The first commit should be to add the tslint.json into the directories with the `lint` script. Next commits will break down to a list of commits to clean up via tslint as a cross between the types fixes:

- use fat arrow over function calls
- use let over var
- add missing semicolons / remove extra semicolons
- format 100 column width
- format spacing indentation

and the folder with the edits:

- examples
- packages/base/src
- packages/base/test
- etc.

So an example commit would be:

> tslint to use fat arrows over function for packages/base/src
>

@remleduff has made a remarkable discovery: on Linux, libuv's clever trick of re-opening the file can actually be done *on anonymous pipes too*, by opening `/proc/self/fd/NUM`. I guess this makes some sense if you recall that FIFOs can be opened multiple times for read and/or write, and anonymous pipes and FIFOs are the same thing, but I was still shocked. (On MacOS there's `/dev/fd/NUM`, but opening this unfortunately seems to just `dup` the existing fd rather than actually re-opening it.)

So this means that technically on Linux I think we actually can handle every common case:

* tty or pipes: re-open
* files on disk: use threads, like any other on disk file
* character devices like /dev/zero or /dev/urandom: re-open probably works? Are character devices ever stateful in such a way that re-opening them gives an fd that acts differently from the original? For example, do character devices ever support seeking? It [looks like they do on FreeBSD](https://www.freebsd.org/doc/en/books/arch-handbook/driverbasics-block.html). But it's easy to test if a fd is seekable with `os.lseek(fd, 0, os.SEEK_CUR)`. Are there any non-seekable files that nonetheless maintain per-open state?
* sockets: re-open fails, but MSG_DONTWAIT works

The first three cases cover the *vast vast vast* majority of stdin/stdout/stderr configurations that actually occur in practice. I'm not sure sockets are common enough to justify a whole extra set of code paths, but maybe.

The one thing that jumped out at me is that the interaction of hostnames and text encoding is stupidly complicated. So is a plain `.decode()` the right thing to do? On Python 2 that meant "use some random system-defined text encoding", which is definitely not right, but it looks like on Python 3 they made it so plain `.decode()` always uses utf-8, which is less wrong... but still probably wrong, because when a hostname is passed as `bytes`, we assume that it's already in the standard hostname "A-label" form, which is required to be all-ascii. Also, for hostnames we guarantee that `bytes` and `str` representations [behave identically if they're all ascii](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/b926f5d0d251d4717026a061b2cd919f3679da35/trio/_socket.py#L228-L235), but for non-ascii characters idna gets involved and who knows what that even does.

So I think the right thing to do here is `.decode("ascii")`. Can you make that change?

And finally, can you add [a newsfragment](https://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/contributing.html#release-notes)? Just create a file called `newsfragments/633.bugfix.rst`, and put a brief description of what was fixed.

This sounds like a compelling addition to the examples in the documentation. I can see the periodic querying for newly added products as a common use case.

However, I don't think adding `skip_products` adds to the useability of `sentinelsat` for two reasons:

- constructing the list of products to skip is something every user has to do on their own anyway - some may keep a database, others a textfile, etc. Currently we can include the functionality to skip existing, full sized downloaded products in a single directory. Including every other way to check for existing products would be a rather complicated endeavour.

- filtering the products list is a single dict comprehension. I don't think hiding that behind a sentinelsat function adds to the goal of being a Pythonic interface to DHuS.

For instance if you have the downloaded products as unzipped folders one could do something like this:

```python

products = api.query()
skip_products = glob.glob("/Sentinel-1/unzipped/S1A*")

filtered_products = {k:v for k,v in products.items() if v['filename'] not in skip_products}
```

That beeing said, we are sorely lacking more examples for common use cases such as yours in the docs and you are more than welcome to contribute :+1:

Ok I see what you mean now. I think it is maybe not clear that the
requirements paragraph in the installation section refers to all the
methods of installations (pip, conda, source) .  We just suggest conda to
install these requirements since some are compiler dependent so it might
not be non trivial to get them correctly installed (for example on mac).

Anyways, it would be nice to be able to just run: pip install velocyto.
However there is the problem that numpy and cython are installation
(build-time not just runtime)  requirements  since they are needed for the
compilation step during installation. Pypi does not support this kind of
requirements (
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/36574463/install-time-dependencies-in-requirements-txt
).
I could of course catch the errors you encountered and use a precompiled C
binary estension in that case, but I guess this sort of operation is not
considered good (or safe) practice (see cython docs) .

If you have a suggestion how to make the docs clearer about this point, you
can just click on the edit on github button on the up right corner and make
your changes, I will review the changes and merge the pull request.
